sanskrit_devanagari:
यदा ते मोहकलिलं बुद्धिर्व्यतितरिष्यति ।
तदा गन्तासि निर्वेदं श्रोतव्यस्य श्रुतस्य च ॥ ५२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yadā te moha-kalilaṁ buddhir vyatitariṣyati |
tadā gantāsi nirvedaṁ śrotavyasya śrutasya ca || 52 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; single danda terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.52।।)
IAST exact (yadā te moha-kalilaṁ buddhir vyatitariṣyati / tadā gantāsi nirvedaṁ śrotavyasya śrutasya ca)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.052 = MBh_06,024.052). Body text identical: 'yadā te mohakalilaṃ buddhir vyatitariṣyati / tadā gantāsi nirvedaṃ śrotavyasya śrutasya ca'. GRETIL writes 'mohakalilaṃ' (one word, anusvara) where modern editions print 'moha-kalilaṁ' (hyphenated); semantic content identical.

anvaya.meaning: thicket / impenetrable mass / dense forest (in compound moha-kalilam: 'thicket of delusion')
you shall come to / you will become
of what is to be heard

translations.verbatim_quote: When your intelligence has passed out of the dense forest of delusion, you shall become indifferent to all that has been heard and all that is to be heard.
When your intellect crosses the quagmire of delusion, you will then acquire indifference to what has been heard and what is yet to be heard (about enjoyments in this world and the next).

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There are many good examples in the lives of the great devotees of the Lord of those who became indifferent to the rituals of the Vedas simply by devotional service to the Lord. When a person factually understands Kṛṣṇa and his relationship with Kṛṣṇa, he naturally becomes completely indifferent to the rituals of fruitive activities.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.52 Yada, when, at the time when; te, your; buddhih, mind; vyatitarisyati, will go beyond, cross over; moha-kalilam, the turbidity of delusion, the dirt in the form of delusion, in the form of non-discrimination, which, after confounding one's understanding about the distinction between the Self and the not-Self, impels the mind towards objects — that is to say, when your mind will attain the state of purity; tada, then, at that time; gantasi, you will acquire; nirvedam, a feeling of disregard for the things heard and yet to be heard.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads moha-kalilam tightly: turbidity-of-delusion in the form of NON-DISCRIMINATION (aviveka) which, after confounding the Self/not-Self distinction, impels the mind towards objects. Crossing this thicket = the mind attains purity (sattva-suddhi). Once purified, the practitioner naturally develops nirveda — a feeling of disregard for the diverse Vedic ends (rituals promising heavenly rewards, etc.) that they have already heard about (śrutasya) and that they may yet hear about (śrotavyasya). The verse names a specific stage in the buddhi-yoga maturation: when discriminating intelligence has crossed the delusion-thicket, the practitioner is no longer captured by the next promised end.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.52 If you act in this manner and get freed from impurities, your intellect will pass beyond the tangle of delusion. The dense impurity of sin is the nature of that delusion which generates attachment to infinitesimal results, of which you have already heard much from us and will hear more later on. You will then immediately feel, of your own accord, renunciation or feeling of disgust for them all.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads moha-kalilam as the dense impurity of sin (pāpa) that generates attachment to alpa-phala (infinitesimal results) — the worldly rewards taught in kāmya-karma sections of the Veda. Crossing the thicket = the intellect passes beyond this tangle. The result is nirveda — a spontaneous renunciation or feeling of disgust for those small results, both those already heard and those yet to be taught. Ramanuja's reading is consistent with Shankara's at the structural level: the verse names a stage of maturation in which the practitioner is no longer captured by the promised-end-pipeline of phala-driven teaching.

positions.rendering: the turbidity of delusion / dirt in the form of delusion / non-discrimination (aviveka) impelling the mind toward objects
the tangle of delusion / dense impurity of sin (pāpa) generating attachment to infinitesimal results

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: kalilam carries a cluster of senses: 'thicket', 'dense mass', 'impenetrable forest', 'turbid water', 'mud'. The image is of a dense obstruction that the buddhi must cross. Prabhupada and Mukundananda render it spatially (forest, quagmire). Shankara reads it through the Advaita lens as aviveka (non-discrimination between Self and not-Self) — a cognitive obstruction. Ramanuja reads it as the impurity of sin generating attachment to small results — an ethical-soteriological obstruction. All four agree on the structural claim: the buddhi crosses something dense, and on the far side comes nirveda — disregard for the chain of promised ends one has heard and would yet hear.

positions.rendering: indifferent to all that has been heard and all that is to be heard
indifference to what has been heard and what is yet to be heard (about enjoyments in this world and the next)
disregard for the things heard and yet to be heard — specifically, the diverse Vedic ends (rituals promising heavenly rewards, etc.)
renunciation or feeling of disgust for the small results (alpa-phala) one has heard much about and will hear more about later

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four traditions agree that the indifference (nirveda) is scoped to a SPECIFIC class of content: the Vedic teachings about phala (worldly and heavenly rewards), promised ends, ritual chains. NOT generic indifference to all hearing/learning. Shankara is most explicit: nirveda = disregard for the diverse Vedic ends. Ramanuja: disgust for the chain of small-result-promises. Mukundananda makes the scoping explicit in a parenthetical ('about enjoyments in this world and the next'). Engineering layer must preserve this scoping carefully — the verse is NOT teaching disengagement from all new inputs; it is naming the saturation that lets the practitioner hear new architecture-meme proposals (microservices! serverless! AI-everything!) without being captured by them.

literal_meaning: When your buddhi will cross the thicket of delusion, then you will come to a state of disregard for what is to be heard and what has been heard.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 2.52 names a specific stage in buddhi-yoga maturation. When the buddhi has crossed the thicket of delusion (moha-kalilam) — read by Shankara as aviveka (non-discrimination between Self and not-Self), by Ramanuja as the impurity of sin generating attachment to small results — the practitioner naturally develops nirveda: indifference scoped specifically to the chain of phala-promising teachings, both those already heard (śrutasya) and those yet to be heard (śrotavyasya). The verse is NOT teaching disengagement from all learning. It is naming a saturation-state in which the practitioner is no longer captured by the next promised end. All traditions read this as a positive maturation, not as cynicism: nirveda here is the natural disinterest of one whose buddhi has matured past the level at which phala-promises operate.

cross_references.explanation: 2.42 named those captured by the 'flowery language of the Vedas' (veda-vāda-rata). 2.52 names the state in which the practitioner has crossed past that capture: nirveda toward śrotavyasya śrutasya ca. The two verses bracket the same psychological dynamic at entry and exit.

2.46 named that for the realised brahmana, the Vedas collapse to the small water-pond before the flood. 2.52 names the cognitive transition that produces this state: when buddhi crosses the moha-kalilam, nirveda toward the chain of teachings arises naturally.

2.53 closes the buddhi-yoga argument with the steady-buddhi condition (samādhau acalā). 2.52 is its precondition: the buddhi must first cross the delusion-thicket; then in 2.53 it stands unmoved in samādhi. The two verses are sequential stages of the same maturation.

remaining_gaps: Abhinavagupta's Kashmir Shaiva (Trika) commentary on this verse not pulled — would offer a different angle on moha-kalilam (typically as māyīya-mala).
